title: Tracheostomy Care and Suctioning
subtitle: The nurse suctions and cares for a tracheostomy safely — suctioning only when needed, hyperoxygenating, using sterile technique, limiting each pass, and keeping emergency equipment at the bedside.

patient.name: Tracheostomy Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse suctions and cares for a tracheostomy using correct technique, oxygenation, and emergency readiness.

vitals.temp: Suction PRN, not by the clock
100% O2 before and after each pass
No suction going in; suction on withdrawal

labs.v: Audible secretions, ↑ work of breathing, desat.
Sterile gloves and catheter in acute care.
No larger than ½ the tube lumen.

nursesNote: The nurse provides tracheostomy care and suctions only when secretions are present (audible gurgling, increased work of breathing, or desaturation) rather than on a fixed schedule. Before and after each suction pass the patient is hyperoxygenated with 100% oxygen. Using sterile technique, the nurse inserts the suction catheter without applying suction until resistance or the premeasured depth is met, then applies intermittent suction while rotating and withdrawing, limiting each pass to 10-15 seconds and allowing recovery between passes. Adult wall suction is set to 100-150 mmHg, and normal saline is not routinely instilled. The nurse monitors SpO2 and heart rate, stopping to reoxygenate if the patient becomes bradycardic or desaturates. Trach ties are secured to a two-finger tightness, and the obturator plus a spare tube (same size and one smaller) stay at the bedside for emergency reinsertion.
providerOrders: Suction the tracheostomy as needed, not on a fixed schedule
Hyperoxygenate with 100% O2 before and after suctioning
Use sterile technique; limit each pass to 10-15 seconds
Keep the obturator and a spare tracheostomy tube at the bedside
Notify the provider for bleeding, persistent desaturation, or dislodgement

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about tracheostomy care and suctioning?

opts.text: Suction the tracheostomy only when secretions are present, not on a fixed schedule
Hyperoxygenate with 100% oxygen before and after suctioning
Apply suction continuously while inserting the catheter
Limit each suction pass to about 10-15 seconds
Routinely instill normal saline before each pass to loosen secretions
Keep the obturator and a spare tracheostomy tube at the bedside
Sterile technique is used to suction a tracheostomy in acute care
Choose a catheter no larger than half the tube's inner diameter
Apply intermittent suction while rotating and withdrawing the catheter
Higher wall pressure (over 200 mmHg) clears secretions faster and is safer
Stop and reoxygenate if the patient becomes bradycardic or desaturates
Two people are recommended when changing tracheostomy ties

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to suction a tracheostomy

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene, don PPE, and set up sterile suction supplies
Hyperoxygenate the patient with 100% oxygen
Insert the catheter without suction to resistance or premeasured depth
Apply intermittent suction while rotating and withdrawing (≤10-15 sec)
Reoxygenate and reassess before any repeat pass
Hyperoxygenating before suctioning
Applying suction while inserting the catheter
Limiting each pass to 10-15 seconds
Routinely instilling normal saline before suctioning
Using sterile gloves and a sterile catheter
Setting adult wall suction to 100-150 mmHg
Tying the trach ties so no fingers fit underneath
Keeping the obturator taped at the bedside

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The tracheostomy is accidentally dislodged — order the response
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay with the patient, stay calm, and call for help
Reinsert the tube using the obturator (or spare same-size tube)
If unable to reinsert, cover the stoma and ventilate with a bag-mask
Apply oxygen and check SpO2 and breath sounds
Notify the provider and document the event
Hyperoxygenating before and after suctioning
Suctioning every hour whether or not secretions are present
Limiting suction passes to 10-15 seconds with recovery between
Instilling normal saline before each pass
Keeping a spare tube and obturator at the bedside
Setting wall suction to 250 mmHg for a thick plug
Stopping and reoxygenating when the patient desaturates
Tying trach ties so tightly no fingers fit underneath

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
